data.title: La Rosa secures $1.25B in financing amid repositioning toward data center infrastructure development
data.description:
La Rosa Holdings (LRHC) announced on Thursday a $1.25B financing, partly to fund its strategic repositioning toward the development of data center infrastructure for AI computing.

The company aims to repurpose and develop high-value properties into advanced data center facilities optimized for artificial intelligence workloads.

Shares were +15.73% pre-market to $4.93.

La Rosa has secured $1.25B in financing facilities from institutional investors, which includes a previously-announced $1B equity purchase facility and a $250M private placement convertible note facility.

The capital infusion is expected to enable the company to pursue strategic acquisitions and joint ventures.

A portion of initial proceeds is intended to be used to serve as a strategic reserve to support growth opportunities.

data.title: Short bets on S&P 500 Healthcare sector rises to 2.00% in October; MRNA stays most shorted stock
data.description:
The S&P 500 Healthcare sector (XLV) experienced a slight rise in average short interest during October, moving from 1.91% to 2.00%. Within this sector, Moderna (MRNA) remains the most heavily shorted stock.

The Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLV), representing the healthcare sector and holding a 12.12% weight in the S&P 500, has seen an 11.09% year-to-date increase, lagging the broader S&P 500 index (SP500) which rose 16.48% during the same period.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

 

Even though short interest in Moderna (MRNA) remains the highest in the sector at 18.21% of its float, down from 18.66% last month, the company is garnering a strong vote of confidence from Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, which assigns a 4.02 out of 5 "Buy" score.

In late October 2025, Moderna's (MRNA) stock surged due to reports of talks with a large biopharma for a potential partnership or buyout provided a major boost to the stock, which had been trading at multi-year lows.

Recently, the company reported Q3 2025 financials that exceeded Wall Street forecasts, despite overall revenue declining sharply year-over-year due to waning COVID vaccine sales. Q3 2025 revenue was $1.0 Billion, beating the consensus, with $971 Million coming from COVID vaccine sales (mainly $781 Million from the U.S.).

The renewed investor optimism is driven by promising cancer vaccine data and surprising findings that the mRNA COVID vaccines may boost cancer survival rates in patients receiving immunotherapy. The company maintains a strong cash position and a commitment to reduced costs and a robust drug pipeline," a Seeking Alpha analyst wrote.

The second-most shorted stock in October was DaVita (NYSE:DVA) at 9.82%, while Henry Schein (NASDAQ:HSIC) was third at 8.44%.

On the other hand, several major companies had very low short interest, including Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) at 0.76%, Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) at 0.81%, STERIS plc (NYSE:STE) at 0.98%, and Merck & Co. (MRK) at 1.04%.

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

 

For the nineteenth consecutive month, the biotechnology industry held the distinction of being the most shorted in the healthcare sector, with short interest rising to 2.97% from 2.86% in October. The healthcare provider and services sector followed with a short interest of 2.30% at the end of October, down from 2.33% in the prior month.

In contrast, the pharmaceuticals' industry recorded the lowest short interest among the healthcare sub-sectors in October, with a short percentage of 1.65% for the month, up from 1.53% in the prior month.

Related ETFs: Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLV), SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF (NYSEARCA:XPH), VanEck Pharmaceutical ETF (NASDAQ:PPH), iShares Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (NASDAQ:IBB), iShares U.S. Pharmaceuticals ETF (NYSEARCA:IHE), and Invesco Nasdaq Biotechnology ETF (NASDAQ:IBBQ).

data.title: Sealed Air may be worth mid-$40s/share in takeover - analyst
data.description:
Sealed Air (SEE) may be worth mid-$40s/share in a takeover after reports on Tuesday that private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice was in talks to take the packaging provider private, according to a Jefferies analyst. 

Shares of Sealed Air (SEE) jumped 21% in premarket trading to $44 on Thursday after the WSJ reported the takeover talks on Wednesday.

"We could see a take-out price in the $40-48/share range, lining up in the 8-9x EV/EBITDA range, not too far off from its historical trading multiple of 9-10x," Jefferies analyst Philip Ng, who has a hold rating and $40 price target, wrote in a note on Wednesday,

data.title: Piedmont Realty Trust launches tender offer for 9.25% senior notes due 2028
data.description:
• Piedmont Realty Trust (PDM) announced a cash tender offer by its operating partnership to repurchase any and all of its 9.250% senior notes due 2028.
  • The notes have $532.46M in principal amount outstanding.
  • The offer references the 3.50% U.S. Treasury due November 15, 2028, with a fixed spread of 95 basis points.

data.title: Novartis says novel antimalarial succeeded in late-stage trial
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) (OTCPK:NVSEF) has announced that its next-generation malaria treatment, KLU156 (GanLum), developed with Swiss nonprofit Medicines for Malaria Venture, reached the primary endpoint in a late-stage trial.

Citing data from its Africa-based KALUMA Phase 3 trial, the Swiss drugmaker said on Wednesday that GanLum met the study’s primary endpoint, performing at a similar level (non-inferiority) to the current standard of care.

Data from KALUMA, as presented at the ongoing American Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene annual meeting 2025, indicated that the combination therapy led to a PCR-corrected cure rate of 97.4% compared to 94.0% for standard of care.

That equated to cure rates of 99.2% and 96.7%, respectively, the company said, citing a per-protocol analysis.

Novartis (NVS) said it will take steps as soon as possible to seek regulatory approvals for GanLum, which received Fast Track and Orphan Drug Designations from the U.S. FDA in 2022.

The company added that if licensed, the antimalarial could be the first major innovation in malaria medications since the approval of artemisinin-based combination therapies, the current gold standard of care, more than two decades ago.
